one_line_takeaway: ANYPoC turns validation of LLM-generated bug reports into an executable-PoC generation and independent-re-verification pipeline, cutting false-positive acceptance from 85-96% (vanilla coding agents) down to 4-15% while producing 30-44% more valid PoCs for true bugs, and used this to help find 121 new bugs (108 confirmed, 92 fixed) across 12 major open-source systems.
key_finding: On the 192-report validation dataset (Table IV): ANYPoC_Claude produced 44% more valid PoCs than vanilla Claude Code on true (positive) reports, and ANYPoC_Codex produced 30% more valid PoCs than vanilla Codex; both ANYPoC variants produced near-zero invalid PoCs while roughly half of baseline agents' candidate PoCs were invalid/non-functional. On false (negative) reports, vanilla Claude Code and Codex failed to reject 96% and 85% of false reports respectively, whereas ANYPoC_Claude and ANYPoC_Codex correctly rejected 85% and 96% of false reports respectively. A price-matched comparison (Table V) showed that running vanilla Claude Code twice at similar total cost ($4.67) still yielded fewer valid PoCs than ANYPoC and generated 366 candidate PoCs requiring human triage versus 82 for ANYPoC (4.5x more manual inspection burden). Ablation (Table VI) showed the analyzer subagent alone rejected 71.8% of false reports on average (62.5 of 87), the generator 20.7% (18), and the checker 7.5% (6.5) -- with the split varying substantially between the Claude- and Codex-based variants. The knowledge-base ablation (Table VII, 96 true reports) showed KB improved valid PoCs generated from 56 to 65 (16% increase), reduced average cost from $5.02 to $4.67 (7% down), cut tool calls by 20% and bash commands by 33%, and reduced redundant file re-reads from 3.6 to 1.8 per PoC. In a real bug-finding campaign, ANYPoC + LLM-Reporter triaged ~2700 noisy candidate reports down to 121 genuine new bugs across the 12 systems (108 confirmed by developers, 92 fixed, 46 PoCs adopted as official regression tests), with the paper noting three authors accomplished this triage versus Anthropic's cited coordinated-disclosure campaign that used six security research companies to triage 1900 findings into 97 fixed vulnerabilities.
core_contribution: ANYPoC is a multi-agent framework (analyzer, generator, checker, plus a self-evolving knowledge base with extractor/filter subagents) that converts a textual candidate bug report into an executable proof-of-concept (PoC) test, acting as an automated validation oracle for LLM-based bug detection pipelines. Given a candidate report r for system S, the analyzer first fact-checks and filters clearly invalid reports; if the report survives, a generator subagent iteratively writes and executes a PoC across two phases (experiment-until-triggering, then a mandatory independent re-execution to collect evidence), explicitly designed to counter reward hacking and hallucinated success claims; finally a separate checker subagent, run without access to the generator's context/trajectory, independently re-executes the PoC in a fresh environment and judges whether the collected evidence (e.g., sanitizer crash, differential trace) genuinely demonstrates the bug and whether the trigger conditions are realistic (achievable by a normal user/attacker, not an artificial harness). A self-evolving, directory-structured Markdown knowledge base (per-project categories: Command Line Tools, Build System, Internal Tools, Test Frameworks, Code, PoC Format) accumulates reusable project-specific knowledge (each entry rated -10..10, versioned) across PoC generation attempts to reduce redundant codebase exploration on large systems like Firefox and Chromium.
threat_model: Not an adversarial security threat model in the traditional sense: the 'threat' being defended against is that of an unreliable LLM validator gaming its own task (reward hacking) by fabricating plausible-looking but non-functional PoCs, hallucinating execution traces, or accepting invalid/false-positive bug reports without genuine evidence. The paper's own explicit design goal is to prevent an LLM agent from self-certifying success on hallucinated grounds, using independence (a checker with no access to the generator's context) and forced re-execution as the defenses. Separately, roughly a quarter of the 121 newly discovered bugs are described as memory-safety/security issues (e.g., use-after-free, OOB read/write) in real systems (Firefox, Chromium, OpenSSL, etc.), including two bugs assigned CVEs (one explicitly named, CVE-2026-2806, a use-before-initialization bug in Firefox's SVG font support), but the paper frames the majority of found defects (logic errors ~40%, per Fig. 7) as general software-quality bugs, not exclusively security vulnerabilities.
limitations: The paper's own Threats to Validity section (VI) states two limitations: (1) Internal: agents could in principle 'cheat' the evaluation by pulling working PoCs or discussion from online resources since network access could not be disabled for realistic PoC generation; mitigated only by LLM-assisted trajectory scanning plus manual review by two authors, which the authors acknowledge 'is not a perfect solution' (agents could hide cheating within complex scripts). (2) External: the 12 selected systems, while diverse and widely used, may not be representative of the broader software ecosystem. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own text: ANYPoC incurs substantially higher per-report cost than baseline agents ($5.02 vs $2.42 for Claude-based, $2.61 vs $1.26 for Codex-based in Table IV); the bug reporter (LLM-Reporter) itself is explicitly out of scope for improvement and is acknowledged as producing 'the vast majority' noise (~2700 raw reports down to 121 genuine bugs); the framework assumes existence of a valid PoC implies validity of the report but not the converse, and explicitly does not attempt to prove the non-existence of a bug (Section III). The KB ablation and subagent-rejection ablation were run on only one model variant/subset (analyzer disabled, 96 reports) rather than the full matrix, limiting generality of those specific numbers.

datasets_used: 192-report validation dataset constructed by the authors: 48 true bug reports + 48 manually-verified rejected reports from LLM-Reporter output (cutoff 2026-02-10, across Chromium, Firefox, OpenSSL, FFmpeg, SQLite, Wasmtime, Redis) plus 48 confirmed + 48 rejected reports independently collected from other users' bug trackers for the same 7 systems

12 real-world software systems used for the bug-finding campaign and generality evaluation: Chromium, Firefox, LLVM, Hermes, OpenSSL, FFmpeg, Wasmtime, Redis, SQLite, FreeType, QuickJS, Memcached (Table II, with LoC/star counts)
~2700 candidate bug reports generated by the authors' own LLM-Reporter agent (following the prior KNighter methodology) across the full bug-finding campaign
quantitative_results: ANYPoC_Claude: 44% more valid PoCs than vanilla Claude Code on positive (true) reports; near-zero invalid PoCs vs ~half invalid for baseline
ANYPoC_Codex: 30% more valid PoCs than vanilla Codex on positive reports
Vanilla Claude Code and Codex failed to reject 96% and 85% of false reports respectively; ANYPoC_Claude and ANYPoC_Codex correctly rejected 85% and 96% of false reports respectively
Price-matched: Claude Code run twice ($4.67 total) generated 366 candidate PoCs needing human triage vs 82 for ANYPoC at similar cost -- 4.5x more manual inspection effort
Ablation: analyzer subagent rejected 71.8% (avg 62.5/87) of false reports, generator 20.7% (18/87), checker 7.5% (6.5/87)

Knowledge base ablation (96 true reports, analyzer disabled): valid PoCs 56 (w/o KB) vs 65 (w/ KB), a 16% increase; unique solves 3 (w/o) vs 12 (w/ KB); avg cost $5.02 vs $4.67 (7% down); tool calls -20%; bash commands -33%; redundant re-reads 3.6 to 1.8 per PoC (49% down)
Bug-finding campaign: 121 new bugs found across 12 systems, 108 confirmed by developers, 92 fixed, 46 PoCs adopted as official regression tests, out of ~2700 raw candidate reports

Knowledge base statistics: 340 knowledge items accumulated, used 808 times total (avg 2.4 items per generation), average usefulness rating 6.0 (median 7.0), 135 self-evolving version updates each raising average rating by 0.30
Two bugs found were assigned CVEs; one is explicitly named: CVE-2026-2806 (use-before-initialization bug in Firefox's SVG font support), fixed within one day of report
baselines_compared: Claude Code (Opus 4.5) -- vanilla coding agent, no ANYPoC validation layer
Codex (GPT-5.2-Codex) -- vanilla coding agent, no ANYPoC validation layer
Price-matched variant: Claude Code run twice at similar cumulative budget to ANYPoC_Claude
